ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301103 covers herbal tea as a category; no specific code exists for canned Chinese liang cha. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0EJW covers herbal infusions broadly; no specific code for canned Chinese liang cha. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 229953004 covers herbal tea as a substance category; no specific code for canned Chinese liang cha.

main_ingredients: traditional Chinese medicinal herbs (e.g. chrysanthemum, honeysuckle, licorice root, prunella vulgaris)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard canned serving of Chinese herbal tea is approximately 310 ml (one can), which is the typical retail can size sold in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Beverages, tea, herbal, brewed (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy); supplemented with branded liang cha product data from USDA Branded Food for canned Chinese herbal tea
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Plain brewed herbal tea (USDA SR Legacy, FDC ID 171917) used as the base. Canned Chinese liang cha (e.g. Wong Lo Kat, Jia Duo Bao) typically contains added sugar at approximately 6–8 g per 100 ml per product label data cross-referenced with USDA Branded Food entries for comparable canned herbal tea products. Energy and carbohydrate values adjusted accordingly. Protein and fat remain effectively zero as confirmed by multiple branded product labels and USDA data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entries for sweetened herbal tea and sugar-sweetened beverages
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for canned Chinese liang cha (herbal tea). GI estimated at 45 based on analogous sweetened herbal tea and lightly sweetened tea beverages from the University of Sydney GI Database. Sweetened teas with moderate sugar content (6–8 g/100 ml) typically fall in the GI range of 40–55 (low category). GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 20.15) / 100 ≈ 9.1, rounded to 9.0. Serving size 310 g × 6.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g = 20.15 g carbohydrate per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (5 mg per 100 ml)
hypertension.guidance: Canned herbal tea contains negligible sodium (~15 mg per can) and poses minimal concern for hypertension; however, individuals should monitor total sugar intake as high-sugar diets can indirectly elevate blood pressure over time.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar content (~6.5 g per 100 ml; ~20 g per can)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Canned Chinese herbal tea contains approximately 20 g of added sugar per 310 ml can, contributing meaningfully to daily carbohydrate load; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit consumption or choose unsweetened herbal tea alternatives to avoid postprandial glucose spikes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero fat, zero cholesterol, low sodium; some herbal ingredients may have antioxidant properties
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Canned herbal tea contains no fat, no cholesterol, and minimal sodium, making it broadly compatible with a heart-healthy diet, though regular consumption of the sweetened canned form should be moderated due to added sugar contributing to metabolic risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Canned Chinese herbal tea (凉茶, liáng chá) is a traditional Chinese medicinal beverage commercially produced and sold in ready-to-drink canned format, widely available across Singapore, Hong Kong, and mainland China. The drink is brewed from a blend of traditional Chinese medicinal herbs such as chrysanthemum flowers, honeysuckle (jīn yín huā), prunella vulgaris (xià kū cǎo), licorice root (gān cǎo), and other cooling herbs, then sweetened with sugar and canned for convenience. Liang cha is rooted in Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) philosophy, where it is consumed to 'clear heat' (清热), reduce internal 'fire' (降火), and relieve symptoms associated with sore throat, dry mouth, and minor inflammation. The flavour profile is mildly sweet with a distinctive herbal bitterness and a cooling aftertaste. The texture is thin and watery, similar to a lightly sweetened tea. Nutritionally, canned liang cha is a low-calorie beverage (~26 kcal per 100 ml) with negligible protein and fat, but contains approximately 6.5 g of added sugar per 100 ml (~20 g per standard 310 ml can). This sugar content is clinically relevant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The beverage is naturally free of allergens, dairy, and gluten. Popular commercial brands in Singapore include Wong Lo Kat (王老吉) and Jia Duo Bao (加多宝). It is commonly consumed as a standalone drink, particularly during hot weather or after consuming 'heaty' foods such as fried or spicy dishes.
common_user_queries: How much sugar is in Wong Lo Kat?
Is herbal tea good for diabetes?
What are the ingredients in Chinese herbal tea?
Is liang cha suitable for diabetics?
Calories in canned Chinese herbal tea
What does liang cha taste like?

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for canned Chinese liang cha exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central plain herbal tea data cross-referenced with USDA Branded Food entries for comparable canned sweetened herbal tea products. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for sweetened tea beverages. Confidence is medium rather than low because multiple converging data points support the nutrient profile.
notes: Protein_g is naturally zero as canned herbal tea is an aqueous herbal infusion containing no protein-bearing ingredients. Fat_g is naturally zero by product formulation; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central and branded product labels. Saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and dietary_fibre_g are confirmed zero for this beverage type. Iron_mg is -1 as it is not reliably reported across sources for this beverage category. Vitamin_c_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this product type. Vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and selenium_mcg are all -1 as these micronutrients are not reported in the primary source for this beverage. Glycemic_index set to 45 (analogous estimate for sweetened herbal tea, low GI category per University of Sydney GI Database conventions); gi_category is low (GI ≤ 55). Serving size of 310 g reflects a standard retail can size common in Singapore. The food_id GD003828 originates from field survey data seed data served by Wan Jia Fu Chinese Restaurant (万家福创新中国菜) at Circular Road. HPB Singapore Foodo does not carry a specific entry for canned liang cha; USDA FoodData Central adapted data used as the best available approved source.

Set glycemic_index to 45 (numeric >0) with gi_category 'low' per GI ≤ 55 threshold rule; documented analogous estimate basis.
Set glycemic_load_per_serving to 9.0 calculated from serving carbohydrate content.
Documented all -1 sentinel values in notes with explicit reason and source reference.
Used only 'exact', 'closest_parent', or 'none' for all ontology match_type fields.
Verified aliases_by_language entries are all present verbatim in flat aliases list.
Populated all mandatory multilingual fields (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) for Singapore/Chinese cuisine context.
